name: Coyolxauhqui
description: Coyolxauhqui, in Aztec mythology, was a powerful and vengeful lunar deity, the daughter of Coatlicue, the Earth goddess, and sister to Huitzilopochtli, the god of war, and the Centzon Huitznahua (the southern stars). Her story is central to understanding the Aztec worldview and their relationship with the cosmos. Coyolxauhqui led her 400 brothers in an attack against their mother, Coatlicue, when they learned she was pregnant under mysterious circumstances – impregnated by a ball of feathers. The siblings considered this a dishonor, and Coyolxauhqui, as their leader, was determined to kill Coatlicue. However, Huitzilopochtli was born fully armed from his mother's womb and swiftly defeated Coyolxauhqui and her brothers. Huitzilopochtli dismembered Coyolxauhqui, scattering her limbs across the hill Coatepec (Snake Mountain). This defeat and dismemberment explain the phases of the moon and the dominance of the sun (Huitzilopochtli) over the night. The Great Temple of Tenochtitlan was constructed to mimic the events of Coatepec, with sacrificial victims thrown down the temple steps to symbolically reenact Coyolxauhqui's fate. Her image, a large stone disk depicting her dismembered body, was placed at the base of the temple, reinforcing the narrative and the power of Huitzilopochtli. Coyolxauhqui embodies the moon, feminine power, rebellion, and ultimately, defeat by the forces of the sun and war. She is a reminder of the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, and the importance of maintaining cosmic order.
subtitle: Moon Goddess, Dismembered Rebel

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Huitzilopochtli
keyMyths.description: Coyolxauhqui led her brothers in an attack against their mother, Coatlicue, but Huitzilopochtli was born and defeated them. This event explains the origin of Huitzilopochtli and Coyolxauhqui's fate.

keyMyths.title: Dismemberment on Coatepec
keyMyths.description: After being defeated by Huitzilopochtli, Coyolxauhqui was dismembered and her body scattered across Coatepec. This symbolizes the phases of the moon and the sun's dominance.
keyMyths.source: Various Aztec codices and archaeological findings

keyMyths.title: The Great Temple Reenactment
keyMyths.description: The Great Temple of Tenochtitlan was built to replicate Coatepec, with sacrifices reenacting Coyolxauhqui's dismemberment. This reinforced the myth and the power of Huitzilopochtli.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of quail, butterflies, and paper were made to Coyolxauhqui, often associated with lunar cycles and female fertility. These offerings were intended to appease her and ensure cosmic balance.

festivals.name: Panquetzaliztli
festivals.description: During Panquetzaliztli, a festival dedicated to Huitzilopochtli, the story of Coyolxauhqui's defeat was reenacted, reinforcing the dominance of the sun (Huitzilopochtli) over the moon (Coyolxauhqui) and the triumph of order over chaos. This re-enactment served as a reminder of the consequences of challenging divine authority.

cultural.modernLegacy: Coyolxauhqui's story continues to resonate in modern Mexican culture, particularly in art and feminist movements. Her image, especially the dismembered sculpture found at the Templo Mayor, symbolizes female empowerment, resistance against patriarchal structures, and a reclaiming of indigenous identity. She is often invoked as a symbol of strength and resilience.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Coyolxauhqui's origin is intertwined with the miraculous birth of Huitzilopochtli. Her mother, Coatlicue, was impregnated by a ball of feathers, an event that angered Coyolxauhqui and her 400 brothers, the Centzon Huitznahua. They considered this immaculate conception a dishonor. Driven by jealousy and a sense of betrayal, Coyolxauhqui incited her brothers to kill their mother. This sets the stage for the central conflict in the myth, highlighting themes of jealousy, betrayal, and the disruption of cosmic order. The story emphasizes the divine and unexpected nature of Huitzilopochtli's birth, contrasting it with Coyolxauhqui's more earthly and vengeful motivations. Her role as the instigator is crucial to understanding the subsequent events and the establishment of Huitzilopochtli's dominance.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The central myth involving Coyolxauhqui revolves around her attempt to murder her mother, Coatlicue. Enraged by Coatlicue's miraculous pregnancy, Coyolxauhqui rallied her 400 brothers to attack her. As they approached Coatepec (Snake Mountain), Huitzilopochtli was born fully armed and ready for battle. He emerged and swiftly defeated Coyolxauhqui, dismembering her body and scattering her limbs across the mountain. This act symbolized the victory of the sun (Huitzilopochtli) over the moon (Coyolxauhqui) and the stars (Centzon Huitznahua). The dismemberment is a key element, representing the lunar cycle and the phases of the moon. The myth explains the relationship between the sun, moon, and stars in the Aztec cosmos, emphasizing the cyclical nature of time and the constant struggle between opposing forces.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: While Coyolxauhqui did not have a widespread cult in the same way as Huitzilopochtli or Tlaloc, she was still venerated and played a significant role in Aztec religious practices. Her image, particularly the massive dismembered sculpture found at the base of the Templo Mayor, served as a constant reminder of Huitzilopochtli's power and the consequences of challenging the divine order. Sacrifices and offerings were made in her honor, often associated with lunar cycles and female fertility. The Templo Mayor itself can be seen as a representation of Coatepec, the site of Coyolxauhqui's defeat, making the temple complex a symbolic reenactment of the myth. The placement of the Coyolxauhqui stone at the base of the temple ensured that every sacrifice performed there symbolically repeated her defeat, reinforcing Aztec cosmology and political power.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Coyolxauhqui's legacy extends beyond her role in Aztec mythology. The discovery of her monumental sculpture in 1978 during excavations at the Templo Mayor in Mexico City was a pivotal moment in Mexican archaeology and cultural identity. The sculpture has become a powerful symbol of pre-Columbian heritage and a source of national pride. Furthermore, Coyolxauhqui has been embraced by feminist scholars and artists as a symbol of female strength, resistance, and the reclaiming of indigenous narratives. Her story is reinterpreted as a critique of patriarchal power structures and a celebration of female empowerment. Her image appears in contemporary art, literature, and political movements, demonstrating her enduring relevance and continued influence in modern Mexican culture and beyond.

shortDescription: Aztec lunar deity and daughter of Coatlicue. She led an attack against her mother and was defeated by her brother Huitzilopochtli.
